institutional_history.description: Federal Constitution Article 3(1): 'Islam is the religion of the Federation, but other religions may be practised in peace and harmony.' Article 11 secures freedom of religion. Article 121(1A) (added 1988) bars civil courts from matters within Syariah jurisdiction.
Article 160(2) defines 'Malay' as a person who 'professes the religion of Islam'. This constitutionally welds the Muslim cleavage to the ethnic-institutional cleavage for the majority community.

Constitution (Amendment) Act 1988 inserts Article 121(1A), establishing parallel jurisdictions. Syariah courts gain finality on personal-law matters for Muslims; civil courts retain federal-list jurisdiction.

Federal Court rulings (Semenyih Jaya 2017, Indira Gandhi 2018, Nik Elin 2024) reassert that State List boundary applies to Syariah jurisdiction; cannot encroach on federal criminal law. Hudud Bills attempted seven times in Kelantan/Terengganu since 1993, zero enacted as federal criminal law.

primary_sources.title: Nik Elin Zurina v Kerajaan Negeri Kelantan (Federal Court, 2024)
Liow, J. Piety and Politics — Islamism in Contemporary Malaysia (Oxford, 2009)

notes: The religious cleavage is jurisdictionally hardwired via parallel-court system since 1988, and existentially welded to the ethnic cleavage for Malays via Article 160(2). The cleavage is salience-time-varying and event-driven: Allah-word case, KK Mart, Bon Odori, pig-farming decree (T4A issue 1999). This is one of the 3R categories — engine modelling is structural/jurisdictional only, never theological.
